61.title: Zemlje i jezici — 12 country-language pairs
61.intro: Welcome to A2! First rule of the new level: country names are capitalized (Njemačka), but language names are ALWAYS lowercase (njemački). The language is really an adjective — the same -ski family you'll dissect tomorrow. Say each pair aloud: country, then language.

items.note: Country capitalized, language lowercase. Always.
You say 'govorim engleski' even if you're American — the language is engleski.
Watch out: the language is talijanski, not 'italijanski'.

61.title: Talking about your languages
61.intro: Three verbs run this whole topic: govoriti (speak), razumjeti (understand), učiti (learn/study). The language goes straight after the verb, lowercase, no preposition: govorim hrvatski. These ten sentences are your template for every 'which languages do you speak?' conversation.

items.hr: Razumijem hrvatski, ali još ne govorim tečno.
items.en: I understand Croatian, but I don't speak fluently yet.
items.note: tečno = fluently; još = still/yet.

items.en: I have been learning Croatian for two years.
items.note: Croatian uses the present tense + a time span: literally 'I learn Croatian two years'.
materinski jezik = native language.
jezike = accusative plural of jezik.

items.hr: Moja žena govori hrvatski, engleski i talijanski.
items.en: My wife speaks Croatian, English and Italian.

items.hr: Učim hrvatski jer je moja žena Hrvatica.
items.en: I'm learning Croatian because my wife is Croatian.
items.note: jer = because; the little word je jumps to right after jer (second position).
After 5 and up: genitive plural — pet jezika, not 'pet jezici'.

61.title: Countries and languages workout
61.intro: Match countries to languages, then produce the sentences yourself. Remember: languages are lowercase, and watch talijanski.

questions.prompt: Which language do they speak in Njemačka (Germany)?
questions.explanation: njemački is the language — lowercase, ending in -ki. Njemačka is the country, Nijemac is a German man.

questions.prompt: How are language names written in Croatian?
options: Capitalized only at the start of a sentence
questions.explanation: Languages are lowercase in Croatian: hrvatski, engleski, njemački. Only the country (Hrvatska) and the person (Hrvat/Hrvatica) are capitalized.

questions.prompt: Ona je iz Francuske. Govori ___.
questions.explanation: The language francuski — lowercase, -ski form. Francuska is the country; Francuskinja is a French woman.

questions.prompt: Write the language they speak in Italija.
questions.explanation: talijanski — this is the trap pair: the country is Italija but the language is talijanski, never 'italijanski'.

questions.prompt: Write the language they speak in Grčka (Greece).
questions.explanation: grčki — note the č. Grčka → grčki, one of the -čki languages like njemački.

questions.prompt: Translate: 'I speak English and I am learning Croatian.'
questions.explanation: Govorim engleski i učim hrvatski. — verb + lowercase language, no preposition needed.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: 'I'm learning Croatian because my wife is Croatian.'
questions.answer: Učim hrvatski jer je moja žena Hrvatica.
questions.explanation: After jer, the clitic je goes immediately into second position of its clause: jer JE moja žena Hrvatica.

questions.prompt: On govori pet ___.
questions.explanation: Numbers 5 and above take the genitive plural: pet jezika. (Compare: dva jezika, tri jezika — but that's a different, dual form; with pet it's true genitive plural.)

62.title: The place-to-adjective factory: -ski, -ški, -čki
62.intro: One suffix family turns any place into an adjective. Base rule: stem + -ski (Split → splitski). But the final consonant reacts: k, c or č + ski fuses into -čki (Amerika → američki), and g or h + ski fuses into -ški (Prag → praški). These adjectives are always lowercase and never form comparatives.

items.note: Drop -ska, add -ski to the stem hrvat-.
Plain consonant + -ski: no change.
Some places take -ački: zagrebački kolodvor (Zagreb station).
k + ski → čki. Same in: Njemačka → njemački.
k → č again, plus the vowel shifts: rijeČki.
g + ski → ški: praŠki.
The suffix works on common nouns too: gradski trg = city square.
morski pas = shark (literally 'sea dog').
t + ski can become -čki/-čka via c: turistički.

62.title: Describe things by origin
62.intro: These adjectives agree with their noun like any other: -ski (masculine), -ska (feminine), -sko (neuter). Instant vocabulary: every country you learned yesterday now describes coffee, films, wine and food.

items.note: kava is feminine → -ska.
film is masculine → -ski.
vino is neuter → -sko.
Dalmacija → dalmatinski.
In the accusative the adjective declines too: talijanskA kava → talijanskU kavu.

62.title: Adjective factory practice
62.intro: Form the adjectives, watch the consonant fusions (k→čki, g→ški), and make them agree with their noun.

questions.prompt: Engleska → the adjective is:
questions.explanation: engleski — lowercase, stem + -ski. Capitalized Engleski would be wrong: these adjectives are always lowercase.

questions.prompt: Which is written correctly?
questions.explanation: pivo is neuter → njemačko, and the adjective is lowercase even though Njemačka (the country) is capitalized.

questions.prompt: Zagreb → the adjective is:
questions.explanation: Zagreb takes -ački: zagrebački kolodvor, zagrebačka katedrala. Not every place is plain -ski — learn city adjectives as words.

questions.prompt: Form the adjective from Split.
questions.explanation: splitski — a plain consonant before -ski stays unchanged: splitska riva, splitski aerodrom.

questions.prompt: Form the adjective from Prag (Prague). Careful: g reacts with -ski.
questions.explanation: g + ski → ški: praški. Same rule for h: Čeh → češki (Czech).

questions.prompt: Translate: 'Italian coffee' (watch the gender agreement).
questions.explanation: kava is feminine, so the adjective takes -ska: talijanska kava.

questions.prompt: Translate: 'Spanish wine' (vino is neuter).
questions.explanation: Neuter agreement: -sko. španjolsko vino, but španjolska glazba (f.) and španjolski jezik (m.).

questions.prompt: Rijeka → the adjective is:
questions.explanation: k + ski → čki, so Rijeka → riječki (riječka luka = the port of Rijeka).

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: 'I drink Italian coffee every morning.'
questions.explanation: In the accusative both adjective and noun take -u: talijanskU kavU. svako jutro = every morning.

63.title: Possessive adjectives: -ov, -ev, -in
63.intro: Croatian turns a person into an adjective to show possession: brat → bratov (brother's). Three endings cover everything: masculine owner + hard consonant → -ov; masculine owner + soft consonant (č, ć, š, ž, j, lj, nj) → -ev; any owner whose name ends in -a → -in. Croatian strongly prefers 'bratov auto' over 'auto od brata'.

items.note: Hard consonant → -ov: bratov auto.
Drop the final -o, add -ov.
ž is a soft consonant → -ev, not -ov.
lj is soft → -ev.
Double change: the fleeting a drops (otac → otc-) and c softens to č → očev.
Owner in -a → -in.
ženina knjiga = my wife's book.
k softens to č before -in: majčin, never 'majkin'.
But baka stays bakin — the k→č change is word-by-word; learn majčin as the exception you'll hear most.
tata is masculine but ends in -a, so it takes -in: tatin auto.

63.title: Whose is it? Agreement in action
63.intro: A possessive adjective agrees with the THING owned, not the owner: bratov auto (m.), bratova kuća (f.), bratovo pismo (n.). The owner only decides -ov/-ev vs -in; the ending after that follows the noun.

items.note: knjiga is feminine → ženinA.
dijete is neuter → sestrinO.
In the accusative the possessive declines like an adjective: ženinA → ženinU.
Inanimate masculine accusative = nominative, so bratov doesn't change here.
The od-construction sounds clumsy; natives reach for the possessive adjective.

63.title: Possessive adjective drill
63.intro: Form the possessives, pick -ov/-ev/-in correctly, and watch the two consonant tricks: otac → očev, majka → majčin.

questions.prompt: brat → the possessive adjective is:
questions.explanation: Masculine owner, hard final consonant → -ov: bratov auto, bratova kuća.

questions.prompt: sestra → the possessive adjective is:
questions.explanation: Owner ending in -a → -in: sestrin. (sestrina is the feminine agreement form — the base adjective is sestrin.)

questions.prompt: Why is it mužev and not 'mužov'?
options: ž is a soft consonant, and soft consonants take -ev
questions.answer: ž is a soft consonant, and soft consonants take -ev
questions.explanation: After soft consonants (č, ć, š, ž, j, lj, nj) the ending is -ev: mužev, prijateljev. After hard consonants: -ov.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Ovo je ___ muž.' (Ana's husband)
questions.explanation: Ana → Anin. Owner in -a takes -in; muž is masculine so no extra ending is needed.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Čitam ___ knjigu.' (my wife's — from žena)
questions.explanation: žena → ženin, then agreement with knjigu in the accusative feminine: ženinU knjigU.

questions.prompt: Form the possessive from otac (father). Two changes happen!
questions.explanation: otac loses its fleeting a (otc-) and c softens to č before -ev: očev. očev auto = father's car.

questions.prompt: Form the possessive from majka (mother). Careful with the k.
questions.explanation: k softens to č before -in: majčin. 'majkin' is a classic learner error.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: 'Ana's husband and Marko's sister work together.'
questions.answer: Anin muž i Markova sestra rade zajedno.
questions.explanation: Anin (owner in -a → -in) agrees with muž (m.); Markova (owner Marko → -ov) agrees with sestra (f.) → MarkovA.

64.title: Accusative across the whole phrase
64.intro: A1 taught you noun endings; A2 makes everything in the phrase agree. In the accusative singular: feminine — every word takes -u (tu lijepu ženu); masculine animate — demonstrative and adjective take -og (or -eg after a soft consonant) and the noun takes -a (tog dobrog čovjeka); masculine inanimate and neuter — nothing changes. Watch each transformation.

items.hr: taj dobar čovjek → tog dobrog čovjeka
items.en: that good man → (I see) that good man
items.note: Animate masculine: -og + -og + -a. Note dobar → dobrog: the fleeting a drops.

items.hr: ovaj novi kolega → ovog novog kolegu
items.en: this new colleague → (I see) this new colleague
items.note: kolega is masculine but declines like an a-noun: adjectives masculine (-og), noun takes -u!

items.hr: moj stari auto → moj stari auto
items.en: my old car → (I see) my old car
items.note: Inanimate masculine: accusative = nominative. Nothing changes.

items.hr: naš novi učitelj → našeg novog učitelja
items.en: our new teacher → (I see) our new teacher
items.note: After a soft consonant (š) the ending is -eg: našEg, but novOg.

items.hr: ta lijepa žena → tu lijepu ženu
items.en: that beautiful woman → (I see) that beautiful woman
items.note: Feminine: -u across the board.

items.hr: ova nova knjiga → ovu novu knjigu
items.en: this new book → (I read) this new book

items.hr: moja dobra prijateljica → moju dobru prijateljicu
items.en: my good friend (f.) → (I see) my good friend

items.hr: to malo dijete → to malo dijete
items.en: that small child → (I see) that small child
items.note: Neuter: accusative = nominative, even for living beings.

64.title: See it, know it, love it — full phrases in sentences
64.intro: The verbs vidjeti, znati, voljeti, čekati, gledati and imati all take a direct object in the accusative. Read each sentence aloud and feel the whole phrase click into place.

items.hr: Vidim ovog novog kolegu svaki dan.
items.en: I see this new colleague every day.
Do you know that good man?
I'm waiting for our new teacher.

items.note: film = inanimate, so the whole phrase stays in nominative form.
jedan declines like an adjective too: jednU dobrU idejU.

items.en: I'm looking for that black coat.

64.title: Whole-phrase accusative drill
64.intro: Decline the full phrase, not just the noun. The two questions everything hinges on: is it feminine (→ all -u)? Is the masculine noun alive (→ -og/-eg + -a)?

questions.prompt: Vidim ___ (taj dobar čovjek).
questions.explanation: Animate masculine: every word changes — tog dobrog čovjeka. Mixing changed and unchanged words is the classic error.

questions.prompt: Vidim ___ (moj stari auto).
questions.explanation: auto is inanimate masculine → accusative = nominative. Nothing changes: Vidim moj stari auto.

questions.prompt: Vidim ___ (ta lijepa žena).
questions.explanation: Feminine accusative: -u on every word — tU lijepU ženU. (toj lijepoj ženi is dative/locative.)

questions.prompt: Put 'ova nova knjiga' into the sentence: Čitam ___.
questions.explanation: Feminine phrase → all three words take -u: ovu novu knjigu.

questions.prompt: Put 'naš novi učitelj' into the sentence: Čekam ___.
questions.explanation: Animate masculine: našEg (soft š → -eg), novOg (hard v → -og), učitelja (-a).

questions.prompt: Put 'ovaj novi kolega' into the sentence: Vidim ___.
questions.explanation: kolega is masculine, so the adjectives take -og — but the noun itself declines like an a-noun: kolegU.

questions.prompt: Vidim ___ (to malo dijete).
questions.explanation: Neuter accusative always equals the nominative — even though a child is alive. The animacy rule applies only to masculine nouns.

questions.prompt: Why is it našEg (not 'našog') novog učitelja?
options: After a soft consonant like š the ending is -eg
Both našeg and našog are standard
questions.answer: After a soft consonant like š the ending is -eg
questions.explanation: Soft-stem words (naš, vaš, moj...) take -eg where hard stems take -og: našeg, ali novog. The following noun doesn't decide this — the stem of each word does.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: 'I see this new colleague every day.'
questions.answer: Vidim ovog novog kolegu svaki dan.
questions.explanation: ovog novog kolegu — masculine agreement on the modifiers, a-noun ending on kolega. svaki dan (every day) stays accusative but inanimate, so unchanged.

65.title: svoj — one's own
65.intro: svoj points back to the subject of the sentence. If the owner IS the subject, Croatian demands svoj — no exceptions, in any person: Čitam svoju knjigu, On vozi svoj auto. Using moj/njegov instead either sounds foreign or changes the meaning completely. svoj declines exactly like moj.

items.note: Subject ja = owner → svoju, not moju.
Owner (she) is NOT the subject (I) → njezinu is correct here.
This is the famous trap: njegov here can only mean someone else's car.
Masculine animate accusative of svoj: svog(a) — just like mog(a).
svoj works for every person: ja, ti, on, mi, vi, oni.
'Volim njegov posao' would mean you love someone else's job.
svoj never describes the subject itself — in the nominative you keep moja.
In commands the hidden subject is ti, so svoj is still required.

65.title: svoj or not svoj?
65.intro: One question decides everything: is the owner the subject of the verb? Yes → svoj. No → moj/tvoj/njegov/njezin.

questions.prompt: 'On vozi svoj auto.' Whose car is he driving?
questions.explanation: svoj always points back to the subject: on = the owner. His own car.

questions.prompt: 'On vozi njegov auto.' Whose car is he driving?
questions.explanation: Since svoj is obligatory for the subject's own things, njegov here can only mean some OTHER man's car. The meaning flips entirely.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Čitam ___ knjigu.' (my own book)
questions.explanation: Subject ja = owner → svoju. 'Čitam moju knjigu' is understood but marked — natives say svoju.

questions.prompt: Fix the possessive: 'Volim moj posao.' Which word should replace moj?
questions.explanation: The subject (ja) owns the job → svoj is obligatory: Volim svoj posao.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Ana voli ___ muža.' (her OWN husband)
questions.explanation: Owner = subject (Ana) → svoj, in the masculine animate accusative: svog(a) muža.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Mi volimo ___ grad.' (our own city)
questions.explanation: svoj works in every person, including mi. grad is inanimate masculine, so the accusative form is just svoj.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is WRONG?
questions.explanation: svoj can never sit in the nominative describing the subject itself — the subject can't 'own itself'. Correct: Moja žena je Hrvatica.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: 'He is reading his own book, not hers.'
questions.answer: On čita svoju knjigu, a ne njezinu.
questions.explanation: svoju = his own (owner = subject on); njezinu = hers (owner ≠ subject). The contrast in one sentence.

66.title: Model talk: Moji jezici
66.intro: Today you produce. Here is a model 90-second talk built entirely from this week's grammar — a -ski adjective, a possessive, a svoj, and the language phrases from day 61. Read it aloud twice, then rebuild it with YOUR facts.

items.en: I also speak a little Spanish.
I've been learning Croatian for two years.
items.note: Fronting hrvatski gives it emphasis — very natural in speech.

items.hr: Učim hrvatski jer je moja žena Hrvatica.
items.en: I'm learning Croatian because my wife is Croatian.

items.note: njezina, not svoja — the owner (she) is not the subject (her family).

items.hr: Želim razumjeti njezinu obitelj — i nju.
items.en: I want to understand her family — and her.

items.hr: Svaki dan učim nove riječi i slušam hrvatsku glazbu.
items.en: Every day I learn new words and listen to Croatian music.
items.note: hrvatskU glazbU — yesterday's -ska adjective in the accusative.

items.hr: Volim svoj mali ritual: kava i deset novih riječi.
items.en: I love my little ritual: coffee and ten new words.
items.note: svoj — because I am the subject and the owner.

items.hr: Još uvijek govorim sporo, ali razumijem puno.
items.en: I still speak slowly, but I understand a lot.
One day I want to speak fluently.
items.note: jednog dana = one day / someday, a fixed genitive time phrase.

66.title: Build your talk
66.intro: Assemble the pieces of the talk. Every question is a sentence you'll actually say out loud afterwards.

questions.prompt: What does 'materinski jezik' mean?
questions.explanation: materinski jezik = native language, from mati/majka (mother). Moj materinski jezik je engleski.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Učim hrvatski ___ je moja žena Hrvatica.' (because)
questions.explanation: jer = because. The clitic je follows it immediately: jer JE moja žena Hrvatica.

questions.prompt: Build the opening line: 'My mother tongue is English.'
questions.explanation: Subject phrase first, je in second position, language lowercase: engleski.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Slušam ___ glazbu.' (Croatian — watch the accusative)
questions.explanation: glazba is feminine and this is the accusative: hrvatskU glazbU. The -ska adjective declines like any adjective.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Želim razumjeti ___ obitelj.' (her family — my wife's)
questions.explanation: The subject is ja, but the owner is she — owner ≠ subject, so njezinu, not svoju. Yesterday's rule, live.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Hrvatski učim dvije ___.' (for two years)
questions.explanation: dvije godine — after dva/dvije the noun takes the -e form: dvije godine, tri godine, but pet godina.

questions.prompt: Build the closing line: 'One day I want to speak fluently.'
questions.explanation: jednog dana (fixed time phrase) + željeti + infinitive. A perfect final sentence for your talk.

questions.prompt: 'Volim ___ mali ritual.' (my own) — which word, and why?
options: svoj — the subject ja is the owner
moj — svoj is only for on/ona
questions.answer: svoj — the subject ja is the owner
questions.explanation: svoj applies in EVERY person whenever subject = owner. ritual is inanimate masculine, so the accusative is plain svoj.

66.title: Deliver the talk to your wife
66.intro: Read your lines aloud as a connected talk — this is your 90-second performance with a live audience of one. Then do it again without looking at the English.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Mogu li ti ispričati svoj mali govor o jezicima?
lines.en: Can I tell you my little talk about languages?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Naravno, slušam te!
lines.en: Of course, I'm listening!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Moj materinski jezik je engleski. Govorim i malo španjolski.
lines.en: My mother tongue is English. I also speak a little Spanish.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: A zašto baš hrvatski?
lines.en: And why Croatian, of all languages?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Učim hrvatski jer je moja žena Hrvatica. Želim razumjeti tvoju obitelj — i tebe.
lines.en: I'm learning Croatian because my wife is Croatian. I want to understand your family — and you.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Bravo! A kako učiš?
lines.en: Bravo! And how do you study?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Svaki dan učim nove riječi i slušam hrvatsku glazbu.
lines.en: Every day I learn new words and listen to Croatian music.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Kada si počeo?
lines.en: When did you start?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Počeo sam prije dvije godine, ali ozbiljno učim od siječnja.
lines.en: I started two years ago, but I've been studying seriously since January.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Govoriš jako dobro!
lines.en: You speak very well!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Hvala! Još uvijek govorim sporo, ali jednog dana želim govoriti tečno.
lines.en: Thanks! I still speak slowly, but one day I want to speak fluently.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ponosna sam na tebe!
lines.en: I'm proud of you!

67.title: Week 9 in ten lines
67.intro: Everything this week taught, one example each. If any line makes you hesitate, that's your weakest unit — redo its exercises today. Say each Croatian line aloud before reading the note.

items.en: I speak English, and I'm learning Croatian.
items.note: Day 61: languages lowercase, straight after the verb.
Day 61: country capitalized, language not.
Day 62: -ski family agrees with its noun — talijanskU kavU.

items.hr: Zagreb → zagrebački, Rijeka → riječki, Prag → praški
items.note: Day 62: k → čki, g → ški.
Day 63: owner in -a → -in, agreement with the thing owned.
Day 63: prefer bratov auto to 'auto od brata'.
Day 64: animate masculine — the WHOLE phrase declines.
Day 64: kolega — masculine adjectives, a-noun ending.

items.hr: Čitam svoju knjigu. / On vozi njegov auto.
items.en: I read my own book. / He drives another man's car.
items.note: Day 65: subject = owner → svoj; njegov then means someone else's.

items.en: How do you say 'weekend' in Croatian?
items.note: Your conversation lifeline for today's chat. (Answer: vikend!)

67.title: Week 9 mixed review
67.intro: All five topics shuffled together — exactly how real speech serves them. No topic labels: you decide which rule each question needs.

questions.prompt: Španjolska → the language is:
questions.explanation: Language = lowercase -ski form: španjolski. Španjolac is a Spanish man.

questions.prompt: Which is the correct adjective from Zagreb?
questions.explanation: Zagreb → zagrebački (with -ački), always lowercase.

questions.prompt: How do natives prefer to say 'my brother's car'?
questions.explanation: The possessive adjective wins: bratov auto. 'auto od brata' is understood but clumsy; bratski means 'brotherly'.

questions.prompt: Put 'taj dobar čovjek' into: Vidim ___.
questions.explanation: Animate masculine accusative: tog dobrog čovjeka — every word declines.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Čitam ___ knjigu.' (my own)
questions.explanation: Subject = owner → svoj, feminine accusative: svoju.

questions.prompt: 'On vozi njegov auto.' Whose car?
questions.explanation: Because svoj is obligatory for one's own things, njegov here necessarily points to a different man.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Ovo je ___ auto.' (my sister's — from sestra)
questions.explanation: sestra → sestrin (owner in -a → -in); auto is masculine so no further ending.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: 'I love Italian coffee and Croatian music.'
questions.answer: Volim talijansku kavu i hrvatsku glazbu.
questions.explanation: Both -ska adjectives agree with their feminine nouns in the accusative: talijanskU kavU, hrvatskU glazbU.

questions.prompt: 'Ana čita njezinu knjigu.' Whose book is Ana reading?
questions.explanation: If it were Ana's own book, Croatian would force svoju. njezinu signals a different female owner — the svoj rule working in reverse.

67.title: Week 9 conversation: languages and learning
67.intro: Your 15-minute conversation starter. Act it out, then keep going off-script — the questions your partner asks here are exactly the ones to keep asking each other. Notice the built-in self-correction on line 6: correcting yourself out loud is a skill, not a failure.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dobro jutro! Danas razgovaramo samo na hrvatskom, može?
lines.en: Good morning! Today we talk only in Croatian, okay?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Može! Jučer sam učio nove riječi cijelo jutro.
lines.en: Deal! Yesterday I studied new words all morning.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Super. Koje jezike sada govoriš?
lines.en: Great. Which languages do you speak now?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Govorim engleski, malo španjolski, a hrvatski još učim.
lines.en: I speak English, a little Spanish, and Croatian I'm still learning.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: A što si radio jučer poslijepodne?
lines.en: And what did you do yesterday afternoon?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Čitao sam moju knjigu... ne, čekaj — svoju knjigu!
lines.en: I was reading my book... no, wait — my OWN book (svoju)!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Bravo! Svoju, jer si ti subjekt. A čija je ona plava knjiga na stolu?
lines.en: Bravo! Svoju, because you're the subject. And whose is that blue book on the table?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: To je ženina knjiga. Tvoja!
lines.en: That's my wife's book. Yours!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Točno! A što voliš više: talijansku kavu ili hrvatsku?
lines.en: Correct! And what do you like more: Italian coffee or Croatian?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Volim talijansku kavu, ali hrvatsku glazbu.
lines.en: I like Italian coffee, but Croatian music.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Odlična kombinacija! Vidim jednog vrlo dobrog studenta.
lines.en: An excellent combination! I see one very good student.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: A ja vidim jednu odličnu učiteljicu.
lines.en: And I see one excellent teacher.

68.title: Weekend plan vocabulary
68.intro: The small talk you'll actually have every Friday: what's happening this weekend? Ten words carry the whole theme. Note the pattern for days: u + accusative — u subotu, u nedjelju.

items.note: Yes, it's a loanword: ovaj vikend = this weekend.
Fleeting a: izlazak → plural izlasci.
Croatia's national weekend institution.
ići na izlet = go on a day trip.
ići u kino = go to the movies.
Imaš li planove za vikend? = Do you have plans for the weekend?
u + accusative for days: u subotu, u petak.

68.title: Futur I in plan sentences
68.intro: Futur I refresher, loaded with weekend content. Short forms: ću, ćeš, će, ćemo, ćete, će. Two spelling rules: infinitives in -ti drop the i before ću (gledat ću), infinitives in -ći keep everything (ići ću). And the clitic ću can never stand first in the sentence.

items.hr: Ići ću na izlet u subotu.
items.en: I'll go on a day trip on Saturday.
items.note: -ći infinitive stays whole: ići ću.

items.en: What will you do on Saturday?

items.hr: Naći ćemo se u centru u sedam.
items.en: We'll meet in the centre at seven.
items.note: naći se = to meet up; ćemo + se cluster together after the verb.

items.en: We'll watch a film in the evening.
items.note: gledati drops its final i in writing: gledat ću, gledat ćemo.

items.en: On Sunday we'll have a barbecue.
items.note: When something else starts the sentence, ćemo slides into second position.

items.en: Friends will come to our place.
items.note: nam = to us (dative clitic) — right after će.

items.en: I'll get in touch with you tomorrow.
items.note: Watch the clitic train: ću + ti + se. Tomorrow's whole lesson hides in this line.

items.hr: Nećemo ništa raditi — odmarat ćemo se.
items.en: We won't do anything — we'll rest.
items.note: Negative future: neću, nećeš, neće... written as one word.
But never 'Ću ići' — the clitic can't open a sentence.

68.title: Weekend futur workout
68.intro: Vocabulary plus futur I mechanics: short forms, spelling, and where ću is allowed to sit.

questions.prompt: What is 'roštilj'?
questions.explanation: roštilj = barbecue. izlet = day trip, odmor = rest, izlazak = night out.

questions.prompt: The short future form for 'ja' is:
questions.explanation: ja ću, ti ćeš, on/ona će, mi ćemo, vi ćete, oni će.

questions.prompt: Which spelling is correct?
questions.explanation: -ti infinitives drop the final i before ću in Croatian: gledat ću. (Gledaću is Serbian spelling; ću can never come first.)

questions.prompt: Complete: '___ ćemo se u centru u sedam.' (We'll MEET...)
questions.explanation: naći se = to meet up. -ći infinitives keep their full form: Naći ćemo se.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'U subotu ___ ići na izlet.' (I will)
questions.explanation: Something else (U subotu) opens the sentence, so the clitic ću takes second position: U subotu ću ići na izlet.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: 'I'll go on a day trip on Saturday.'
questions.answer: Ići ću na izlet u subotu.
questions.explanation: Verb-first future: Ići ću... The clitic ću lands in second position, right after the infinitive.

questions.prompt: Complete the negative: 'On ___ raditi u nedjelju.' (won't)
questions.explanation: Negative futur I is one word: neću, nećeš, neće, nećemo, nećete, neće. It CAN start a sentence because it's stressed.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: 'On Sunday we'll have a barbecue.'
questions.explanation: U nedjelju fills first position, so ćemo must come immediately after it — before the infinitive.

68.title: Plan the real weekend
68.intro: Plan your actual upcoming weekend with your wife — start from the script, then swap in your real plans. Every 'Me' line uses futur I; keep that going when you improvise.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Što ćemo raditi ovaj vikend?
lines.en: What will we do this weekend?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: U subotu ujutro radit ću u vrtu, a poslije sam slobodan.
lines.en: On Saturday morning I'll work in the garden, and after that I'm free.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Super! Hoćemo li ići na izlet?
lines.en: Great! Shall we go on a day trip?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Može! Ići ćemo u planine ako bude sunca.
lines.en: Sure! We'll go to the mountains if it's sunny.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: A navečer? Marko i Ana nas zovu na roštilj.
lines.en: And in the evening? Marko and Ana are inviting us to a barbecue.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Odlično! Doći ćemo u sedam. Što ćemo donijeti?
lines.en: Excellent! We'll come at seven. What will we bring?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Donijet ćemo vino i salatu. A u nedjelju?
lines.en: We'll bring wine and a salad. And on Sunday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: U nedjelju nećemo ništa raditi — odmarat ćemo se cijeli dan.
lines.en: On Sunday we won't do anything — we'll rest all day.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Savršen plan! Hoćemo li gledati film navečer?
lines.en: A perfect plan! Shall we watch a film in the evening?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Da, gledat ćemo jedan dobar hrvatski film.
lines.en: Yes, we'll watch a good Croatian film.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dogovoreno! Javit ću Ani da dolazimo.
lines.en: Agreed! I'll let Ana know we're coming.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Super. Veselim se vikendu!
lines.en: Great. I'm looking forward to the weekend!

69.title: me, te, ga, je — the object pronoun pairs
69.intro: Every accusative pronoun has two forms: a stressed one (mene, tebe, njega) and a short clitic (me, te, ga). The clitic is the default — natives use it 95% of the time — and it always sits in second position. The stressed form appears after prepositions, for contrast, and as a stand-alone answer.

items.note: The everyday clitic is je; use ju when it would bump into the auxiliary je: Vidio JU JE jučer.
Same letters stressed and clitic — only the stress differs in speech.
After a preposition: ALWAYS the stressed form. 'za me' is impossible in standard speech.

items.en: Who are you waiting for? — Him.
items.note: A one-word answer must be stressed: Njega, not 'Ga'.
The clitic hugs second position: Vidim te. Sutra te vidim.

69.title: Question and answer: replace the noun
69.intro: The core move of natural Croatian: once a person or thing is named, it becomes a clitic. Read each mini-exchange aloud — question with the noun, answer with the pronoun.

items.en: Do you see Marko? — I see him.
Do you know Ana? — I know her.
Are you waiting for me? — I'm waiting for you!
items.note: The pronouns swap perspective: your me is my te.

items.en: Do you see us? — I see you.

items.hr: Gledaš li taj film? — Gledam ga.
items.en: Are you watching that film? — I'm watching it.
items.note: ga also means 'it' for masculine things like film.

items.hr: Voliš li ove pjesme? — Volim ih.
items.en: Do you like these songs? — I like them.

items.note: Preposition za → stressed tebe.
ju instead of je, because the auxiliary je follows immediately.

69.title: Pronoun replacement drill
69.intro: Answer with clitics, reach for stressed forms after prepositions and in one-word answers. Speed is the goal — these must become reflexes.

questions.prompt: Which is the CLITIC (short) form for 'him'?
questions.explanation: njega/ga — ga is the short everyday form. (mu is dative 'to him' — different case.)

questions.prompt: Vidiš li Marka? — The natural answer is:
questions.explanation: Clitic by default: Vidim ga. 'Vidim njega' adds heavy emphasis (HIM, not someone else), and a clitic can never start the sentence.

questions.prompt: 'This is for ___.' (me) — Ovo je za ___.
questions.explanation: After a preposition the stressed form is obligatory: za mene, kod mene, bez mene.

questions.prompt: Answer with a pronoun: 'Poznaješ li Anu?' — 'Poznajem ___.'
questions.explanation: Ana → je (the standard feminine accusative clitic). ju is reserved for when the auxiliary je follows.

questions.prompt: Answer with a pronoun: 'Vidiš li nas?' — 'Vidim ___.'
questions.explanation: They asked about 'us', you answer about 'you (plural)': Vidim vas. Perspective flips with pronouns.

questions.prompt: Answer with a pronoun: 'Voliš li ove pjesme?' — 'Volim ___.'
questions.explanation: Plural things → ih (them): Volim ih. Stressed version: njih.

questions.prompt: 'Koga čekaš?' You answer with ONE word:
questions.explanation: A clitic can't stand alone — a one-word answer needs the stressed form: Njega. (On is nominative — wrong case.)

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Vidio ___ je jučer.' (her)
questions.explanation: Right next to the auxiliary je, the feminine clitic switches to ju: Vidio ju je jučer — avoiding the awkward 'je je'.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: 'I see him every day on the tram.'
questions.answer: Vidim ga svaki dan u tramvaju.
questions.explanation: The clitic ga takes second position, straight after the verb: Vidim ga...

70.title: The clitic chain: li → aux → DAT → ACC → se → je
70.intro: All the little unstressed words gather in SECOND position of the clause, in one fixed internal order: the question particle li, then the auxiliary (sam, ću, bih...), then the dative clitic, then the accusative one, then se — and the auxiliary je, uniquely, goes last of all. Memorize the chain as a template; every example below is the chain in action.

items.hr: li → sam/ću/bih → mi/ti/mu/joj → me/te/ga/je → se → je
items.en: the fixed chain — question word, auxiliary, dative, accusative, se, and je dead last
items.note: This order NEVER varies. One rule, hundreds of correct sentences.

items.en: Yesterday I gave it to him.
items.note: aux (sam) → dative (mu) → accusative (ga).

items.en: I gave it to him yesterday.
items.note: Different first word, same rule: the cluster sits right after whatever comes first.
je jumps BEHIND the dative: mi je, never 'je mi'.
The full chain: dative (mi) → accusative (ga) → je last.

items.en: Did you give it to him?
items.note: In questions with jesi, li comes first in the cluster: li → mu → ga.

items.en: Can you send it to me?
items.note: li right after the verb, then dative, then accusative.

items.en: I'll get in touch with you tomorrow.
items.note: aux (ću) → dative (ti) → se.
When se is present, the auxiliary je DROPS in the 3rd person: not 'mu se je nasmijala'.
Feminine ju before je — yesterday's rule feeding into today's chain.

70.title: Clitic order under the microscope
70.intro: Six scrambles and the logic behind them. Say each solved sentence aloud twice — rhythm is how natives store this rule.

questions.prompt: In the clitic chain, what comes immediately after li?
questions.explanation: The chain: li → aux → dative → accusative → se → je. li opens, je closes.

questions.prompt: Which is correct for 'She gave it to me'?
questions.explanation: dative (mi) before accusative (ga), and je always last. je can also never start the sentence.

questions.prompt: Which order is correct inside the cluster: dative or accusative first?
options: Dative first: mi ga, ti je, mu ga
Accusative first: ga mi, je ti
questions.answer: Dative first: mi ga, ti je, mu ga
questions.explanation: Always dative → accusative: Daj MI GA (give me it), Poslao TI JE pismo... the receiver comes before the thing.

questions.prompt: Unscramble: 'Yesterday I gave it to him.'
questions.explanation: Jučer takes first position; then the whole cluster in chain order: sam (aux) → mu (dat) → ga (acc).

questions.prompt: Unscramble: 'She gave me the book.'
questions.explanation: The participle opens, then mi (dative) BEFORE je — because je always goes last in the cluster.

questions.prompt: Unscramble the question: 'Did you give it to him?'
questions.explanation: Jesi carries the stress, then the chain: li → mu (dat) → ga (acc). The participle dao closes.

questions.prompt: Complete with TWO clitics in the right order: 'Možeš li ___ ___ poslati?' (to me + it)
questions.explanation: Dative before accusative: mi ga. Full sentence: Možeš li mi ga poslati? — Can you send it to me?

questions.prompt: Unscramble: 'I'll get in touch with you tomorrow.'
questions.explanation: aux (ću) → dative (ti) → se, exactly as the chain predicts. se comes after all the pronoun clitics.

questions.prompt: Unscramble the polite question: 'Can you send it to me?'
questions.explanation: li → mi → ga: the question particle leads the cluster, then dative, then accusative. This exact sentence is day 99's speed drill — bank it now.

questions.prompt: 'Ona mu se nasmijala.' Why is there no 'je'?
options: With se, the 3rd-person auxiliary je is dropped
It's a mistake — je is required
questions.answer: With se, the 3rd-person auxiliary je is dropped
questions.explanation: se + je collapses to just se in the 3rd person singular: Ona mu se nasmijala, Ona se vratila. Everywhere else the auxiliary stays: Nasmijala sam mu se.
